color_semantics.TEAL: quantum reality (probability cloud, P(r) curve, mean radius, distribution)
color_semantics.CRIMSON: classical orbit picture (Bohr circle, single radius, wrong picture)

metadata.note: Card 06. Exclusions: no radial Schrödinger derivation, no ansatz/normalization algebra, no SO(4) symmetry, no spherical-harmonic machinery.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.prompt: In Bohr's model, the ground-state electron sits on a circle at the Bohr radius a₀ = 0.053 nm. One radius. Most probable equals average equals orbit radius.

beats.prompt: The radial probability density is P(r) = r² e^{−2r/a₀} times a constant. It starts at zero, peaks somewhere, then decays — not a spike at one radius.

beats.label: Where does P(r) peak? Take the derivative, set it to zero. The most probable radius is exactly a₀. B

beats.prompt: The mechanism: the distribution has a tail.
beats.label: The mechanism: the distribution has a tail.

beats.prompt: P(r) has a long tail at large r. The average — the integral of r times P(r) — weighs the tail. The result: ⟨r⟩ = 3a₀/2, sitting beyond the peak at a₀.

beats.prompt: Two different numbers — a₀ for the peak, 1.5a₀ for the mean. A circle cannot do that. Two different numbers is the mathematical proof that there is no orbit.

beats.prompt: An orbit would give one number. A cloud gives two.
beats.label: An orbit would give one number. A cloud gives two.

beats.label: The quantum cloud is spherically symmetric — no preferred direction. The 90%-probability isosurface

beats.prompt: Bohr got the energies right because a₀ is a real length scale in the exact solution. But the orbit is a fiction. The length scale is real. The circle is not.
